title: Trauma and the Body's Foundation: Building Safety From the Ground Up
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the body’s foundation and learn science-based methods to build safety from the ground up for lasting healing.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

Many people approach trauma as a psychological problem—a set of distressing memories or emotions trapped in the mind. Yet decades of research, including pivotal work by Bessel van der Kolk, reveal trauma as a profound bodily experience. The body holds the imprint of trauma long after conscious memory fades or therapy stalls. Healing trauma, therefore, requires building safety within the body’s foundation, not just the mind.

Van der Kolk’s landmark book The Body Keeps the Score underscores how trauma reorganizes the nervous system, altering how the body perceives safety and threat. This means trauma is not just in your head; it’s in your muscles, breath, posture, and nervous system responses. When you feel stuck after therapy, it is often because the body’s implicit, somatic memory remains unaddressed.

What Is the Body’s Foundation?

The body’s foundation refers to the core systems that support our sense of safety and presence: the nervous system, the musculoskeletal system, and the breath. These systems work together to regulate arousal and maintain homeostasis. When trauma disrupts this foundation, the body becomes either hypervigilant or shut down, making it hard to feel safe or grounded.

Somatic psychologist Pat Ogden highlights the importance of the body’s foundational layers, particularly the role of the lower body—pelvis, legs, feet—in providing a physical sense of grounding and support. Without this, it’s difficult to develop internal safety or resilience.

The Nervous System as the Foundation

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory is essential for understanding the nervous system’s role. Porges explains how the autonomic nervous system toggles between states of social engagement, fight-or-flight, and shutdown. Trauma can lock the system into defensive states, impairing the ability to engage socially or feel calm and safe.

According to Deb Dana, a leading clinician applying Polyvagal Theory, rebuilding safety starts with recognizing and calming the nervous system’s defensive states. This requires bottom-up approaches focused on bodily sensations rather than top-down cognitive strategies alone.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Isn’t Enough

Many who have tried traditional therapy find themselves stuck because talk therapy primarily targets the prefrontal cortex—the thinking brain. Trauma, however, is frequently stored in the limbic system and brainstem, areas responsible for survival and emotional memory.

Francine Shapiro’s work with Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) shows how integrating body-based processing can unlock stuck trauma. Still, even EMDR benefits from incorporating somatic awareness to fully access the body’s implicit memories.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing®, emphasizes that trauma is held in physical tension patterns and nervous system dysregulation. His approach uses body awareness and tracking sensations to renegotiate trauma responses safely.

Building Safety From the Ground Up: Practical Steps

Reestablishing safety in the body’s foundation involves deliberate, gentle engagement with the body and nervous system. Here are evidence-based strategies:

  • Grounding Through the Feet and Legs: Engage in practices that connect you physically to the ground—standing barefoot, feeling the weight in your feet, or gentle leg stretches. Pat Ogden notes that this can restore a sense of physical support and stability.
  • Breath Regulation: Breath is the bridge between the autonomic nervous system and conscious control. Slow, diaphragmatic breathing can shift the nervous system out of fight-or-flight states (Deb Dana).
  • Movement and Sensory Awareness: Gentle, mindful movement—like yoga, Qi Gong, or somatic exercises—helps renegotiate the body’s trauma responses by activating the parasympathetic nervous system (Peter Levine).
  • Tracking Sensations: Learning to notice and describe bodily sensations creates a window for nervous system regulation and integration (Pat Ogden).
  • Safe Relational Engagement: Social engagement cues—eye contact, voice tone, facial expressions—stimulate the vagus nerve’s social engagement system, promoting safety (Stephen Porges).

Why Safety Is Not a Given for Trauma Survivors

For many trauma survivors, the baseline experience of safety is elusive. The nervous system may be chronically dysregulated, leading to hyperarousal, dissociation, or shutdown. As Gabor Maté explains, trauma rewires the nervous system’s default state, making what should be safety feel threatening.

Safety is not just external—it must be internalized. This means cultivating a felt sense of safety inside the body, which requires patience and consistent somatic practice.

"Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you." – Gabor Maté

Integrating the Internal Family Systems Model

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic approaches by helping survivors identify and relate compassionately to different internal parts—especially those holding trauma. When combined with somatic awareness, IFS can help rebuild internal safety by harmonizing conflicted parts and calming protective responses.

Using IFS with somatic work deepens the connection to bodily sensations and the nervous system, promoting integration and healing from the ground up.

Conclusion: Healing Starts at the Foundation

Trauma recovery is not a linear journey and often stalls when the body’s foundational systems remain dysregulated. Research by van der Kolk, Levine, Porges, and others makes it clear: trauma lives in the body, and healing must begin there.

Building safety from the ground up means reconnecting with your body’s foundational systems—nervous system, musculoskeletal support, and breath—and cultivating a felt sense of safety that can reshape your nervous system’s response to the world.

For those who have tried therapy but still feel stuck, incorporating somatic practices informed by research offers a powerful path forward. Your body is not just a vessel for trauma; it is the key to your healing.

faqs.q: Why does trauma affect the body so deeply?
faqs.a: Trauma impacts the nervous system and body tissues, creating patterns of tension and dysregulation that persist beyond conscious memory. The body holds implicit memories of trauma that influence how you feel and react.

faqs.q: How is somatic healing different from traditional therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic healing focuses on bodily sensations and nervous system regulation rather than just thoughts or talk. It helps renegotiate trauma responses stored in the body, which traditional talk therapy may not fully address.

faqs.q: What role does the nervous system play in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: The nervous system governs your responses to safety and threat. Trauma can lock it into defensive states. Recovery involves calming and regulating these states to restore balance and a sense of safety.

faqs.q: Can I do somatic healing on my own safely?
faqs.a: Yes, many somatic practices like grounding, breath work, and mindful movement can be done safely on your own. However, working with a trained professional is recommended if trauma responses feel overwhelming.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and offers practical insights for healing through body-based approaches.
